result.markdown:
--- title: "Choose your columns with selectors" format: html: toc: true revealjs: slide-number: true toc: false code-fold: false code-tools: true --- ## Introduction to Selectors Complex column selection beyond simple name lists: ::: {#f4da8bc7 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import skrub.selectors as s ``` ::: ## Selecting by Data Type Available selectors: - `.numeric()`: Numeric columns (int or float) - `.integer()`: Integer columns only - `.float()`: Floating-point columns - `.string()`: String columns - `.categorical()`: Categorical columns - `.any_date()`: Date or datetime columns - `.boolean()`: Boolean columns ## Example: Select String Columns ::: {#70e0e705 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import SelectCols import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ "age": [25, 30, 35], "name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"], "city": ["NYC", "LA", "Chicago"] }) SelectCols(cols=s.string()).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
name city
0 Alice NYC
1 Bob LA
2 Charlie Chicago
``` ::: ::: ## Selecting by Characteristics - `.all()`: All columns - `.has_nulls()`: Columns with at least one null - `.cardinality_below(threshold)`: Few unique values ::: {#3ddf5cd8 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} SelectCols(cols=s.has_nulls()).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
0
1
2
``` ::: ::: ## Selecting by Name - `.cols("name")`: Specific column name(s) - `.glob("pattern*")`: Unix shell-style globbing - `.regex("pattern")`: Regular expressions ::: {#f7b83465 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} SelectCols(cols=s.glob("metric_*")).fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
0
1
2
``` ::: ::: ## Combining Selectors {.smaller} Use logical operators: ::: {#7dea5f27 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # OR: datetime columns OR string columns s.any_date() | s.string() # AND: string columns without nulls s.string() & ~s.has_nulls() # Exclude: all columns except "datetime-col" s.all() - "datetime-col" # Inverse: NOT numeric ~s.numeric() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` (~numeric()) ``` ::: ::: ## Extracting Selected Columns Get the list of selected columns: ::: {#fa8f5d87 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} selector = s.has_nulls() columns_with_nulls = selector.expand(df) # Use in dataframe operations df.drop(columns=columns_with_nulls) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
age name city
0 25 Alice NYC
1 30 Bob LA
2 35 Charlie Chicago
``` ::: ::: ## Custom Selectors: Filter by Condition ::: {#d0f528d6 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} def more_nulls_than(col, threshold=0.5): return col.isnull().sum() / len(col) > threshold selector = s.filter(more_nulls_than, threshold=0.5) ``` ::: ## Custom Selectors: Filter by Name ::: {#db822b5c .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} selector = s.filter_names(lambda name: name.endswith('_mm')) s.select(df, selector) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
0
1
2
``` ::: ::: ## Key Takeaways - Use selectors for flexible, reusable column selection - Combine selectors with logical operators - `expand()` extracts selected column names - Create custom selectors for domain-specific logic - Perfect with `ApplyToCols`, `SelectCols`, `DropCols`
